coredata.dc:title: An electrochemiluminescence sensor based on Fe/Zn-BTC@C-dots sensitisation for thiamethoxam detection
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Shuhuai
Deng, Yan
Pang, Chaohai
Ma, Xionghui
Wu, Yuwei
Wang, Mingyue
Li, Jianping
Xu, Zhi
Zhang, Lianming
coredata.dc:description: The performance of an electrochemiluminescent reagent directly affects the performance of its corresponding electrochemiluminescent (ECL) sensor. In this study, Fe/Zn-benzene-1,3,5-tricarboxylic acid metal–organic framework@C-dots (Fe/Zn-BTC@C-dots), which consist of a nano-loaded carbon quantum dot material with an excellent luminescence performance, were synthesised for the first time. Subsequently, they were used to modify the surface of a glassy carbon electrode, and combined with a single-chain thiamethoxam DNA aptamer to construct a biosensor. A novel method for the detection of thiamethoxam was established based on control of the excellent luminescence signal of the Fe/Zn-BTC@C-dots by specific recognition of the aptamer by the target molecule (a signal switch). Upon capturing thiamethoxam, the electrochemical luminescence intensity of the sensor was quenched. The ability of Fe/Zn-BTC to effectively catalyse and amplify the electrochemical luminescence of the C-dots significantly improved the sensitivity of the sensor, while the specific recognition of the target by the aptamer imparted the sensor with an excellent selection ability. Such an advanced approach is promising for the detection of thiamethoxam, with a linear range of 5–5000 × 10−11 mol/L and a sensitivity of 9.81 × 10−12 mol/L being achieved. Moreover, the sensor exhibited recovery rates of 90.5–109.3% upon the detection of thiamethoxam in actual samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fe/Zn-BTC
Carbon quantum dots
DNA aptamer
Thiamethoxam
Electrochemiluminescence

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by the Hainan Province Science and Technology Special Fund ( 320QN302 and ZDYF2022XDNY232 ), the earmarked fund for CARS ( CARS-31 ), the Key Laboratory of Tropical Fruits and Vegetables Quality and Safety for State Market Regulation ( ZX-2023002 ), and the Central Public-interest Scientific Institution Basal Research Fund ( 1630082020001 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration showing thiamethoxam detection by the newly developed ECL sensor.
SEM images of the (A) Fe-BTC and (B) Fe/Zn-BTC@C-dots species. (C) XRD pattern and (D) XPS profile of the prepared Fe/Zn-BTC@C-dots.

(A) CV curvesand (B) EIS curves of the sensor, as acquired under different conditions: a) The bare GCE electrode; b) the Fe/Zn-BTC@C-dot-modified electrode; c) the Fe/Zn-BTC@C-dots/aptamer-modified electrode; and d) the Fe/Zn-BTC@C-dots/aptamer-modified electrode after capturing 5 × 10−9 mol/L of thiamethoxam.

(A) ECL responses of the sensor under different conditions: a) The C-dots-modified electrode; b) the Fe/Zn-BTC-modified electrode; c) the Fe-BTC@C-dots-modified electrode; d) the Zn-BTC@C-dots-modified electrode; and e) the Fe/Zn-BTC@C-dots-modified electrode. f) The quenching effect of a 2 × 10−8 mol/L thiamethoxam solution upon contact with the Fe/Zn-BTC@C-dots-modified electrode. (B) Effect of the H2O2 concentration on the current responses of a. the C-dots-modified electrode; b. the Zn-BTC@C-dots-modified electrode; c. the Fe-BTC@C-dots-modified electrode; and d. the Fe/Zn-BTC-modified electrode.
(A) ECL response of the sensor to thiamethoxam at various concentrations: a–j) 0, 5, 20, 100, 500, 1500, 2000, 3000, 4000, and 5000 × 10−11 mol/L, respectively. (B) Calibration curve.

Variation in the ECL of the developed sensor in the presence of a 5 × 10−9 mol/L thiamethoxam solution containing 5 × 10−7 mol/L interferents: a) imidacloprid, b) acetamiprid, c) tiothiamine, d) fipronil, e) oxytetracycline, f) chloramphenicol, g) atrazine, h) chlorpyrifos, i) methyl parathion, j) acetamiprid, k) nitenpyram, and l) thiacloprid.
Results of the sample assays and recovery analyses.

head.ce:title: An electrochemiluminescence sensor based on Fe/Zn-BTC@C-dots sensitisation for thiamethoxam detection

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Shuhuai Li and Yan Deng contributed equally to this work

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The performance of an electrochemiluminescent reagent directly affects the performance of its corresponding electrochemiluminescent (ECL) sensor. In this study, Fe/Zn-benzene-1,3,5-tricarboxylic acid metal–organic framework@C-dots (Fe/Zn-BTC@C-dots), which consist of a nano-loaded carbon quantum dot material with an excellent luminescence performance, were synthesised for the first time. Subsequently, they were used to modify the surface of a glassy carbon electrode, and combined with a single-chain thiamethoxam DNA aptamer to construct a biosensor. A novel method for the detection of thiamethoxam was established based on control of the excellent luminescence signal of the Fe/Zn-BTC@C-dots by specific recognition of the aptamer by the target molecule (a signal switch). Upon capturing thiamethoxam, the electrochemical luminescence intensity of the sensor was quenched. The ability of Fe/Zn-BTC to effectively catalyse and amplify the electrochemical luminescence of the C-dots significantly improved the sensitivity of the sensor, while the specific recognition of the target by the aptamer imparted the sensor with an excellent selection ability. Such an advanced approach is promising for the detection of thiamethoxam, with a linear range of 5–5000 × 10−11 mol/L and a sensitivity of 9.81 × 10−12 mol/L being achieved. Moreover, the sensor exhibited recovery rates of 90.5–109.3% upon the detection of thiamethoxam in actual samples.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration showing thiamethoxam detection by the newly developed ECL sensor.

A highly selective and amplification strategy thiamethoxam sensor using an electrode modified with a Fe/Zn-BTC@C-dots and a DNA aptamer. Fe/Zn-BTC effectively amplify the ECL of the C-dots improved the sensitivity of the sensor, while the specific recognition of the target by aptamer imparted the sensor with an excellent selection ability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Thiamethoxam [1], which is a neonicotinoid pesticide with a high efficiency and a broad-spectrum activity, has been widely employed in the farming of numerous crops, including tomatoes, peppers, and bananas. Although thiamethoxam is considered a moderately toxic and relatively safe pesticide, several studies have shown that the presence of thiamethoxam residues in agricultural products and production environments can be detrimental to human health and that their toxicity may be underestimated. Hence, determining the thiamethoxam residues present in agricultural products and production environments is crucial. Currently, the analytical methods employed for thiamethoxam detection are mainly based on high-performance liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [2,3], electrochemical methods [4,5], and fluorescence methods [6,7]. However, the use of these techniques is largely limited owing to their high instrumental costs, the requirement for specialised LC-MS devices, their poor electrochemical stabilities, and the low sensitivities of the colorimetric methods. Therefore, the development of a simple and reliable sensing platform with high selectivity and sensitivity is required for the detection of thiamethoxam.

Recently, the electrochemiluminescence (ECL) sensing technology [8,9] has become an attractive candidate for pesticide residue analysis owing to its numerous technical advantages, such as a low background level, a high signal-to-noise ratio, a high sensitivity, and its facile modulation. As the performance of the electrochemiluminescent reagent determines the detection ability of the corresponding ECL sensor, the lack of efficient and stable ECL materials has become a major constraint for the further development of the ECL sensing technology [10]. Conventionally, organic ECL luminescent materials [11], luminols [12,13], and metal quantum dots [14,15] have faced numerous drawbacks associated with a low luminous efficiency, a strong environmental sensitivity, and a high biological toxicity. Therefore, the preparation of ECL reagents that exhibit a high luminous efficiency and a low biotoxicity is required, and the establishment of a corresponding biosensor platform is necessary to achieve the highly sensitive detection of thiamethoxam.

To date, various novel functional materials that can further enhance the performances of electrochemiluminescent reagents have been reported. For example, carbon dots (C-dots) [16], which are zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with ECL properties, have recently attracted substantial attention in the field of sensing owing to their excellent water solubility, low toxicity, environmental friendliness, and excellent biocompatibility. However, the low quantum yield and weak ECL intensity associated with C-dots limit their application in ECL sensing platforms [17]. To address this issue, the introduction of additional functional materials to construct composite C-dots materials could be employed to enhance electron transfer during ECL. In this context, Kamyabi. et al. [18] constructed CuS/C-dots/g-C3N4 composite-modified electrode sensors using graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) nanosheets as a support material for the C-dots, ultimately achieving C-dots amplification of the ECL intensity.

In addition, metal–organic framework (MOF) materials [19,20] have received growing attention in the field of ECL. As a class of crystalline porous materials with periodic network structures that are formed by the self-assembly of inorganic metal centres and bridged organic ligands, MOFs possess large specific surface areas, high porosities, and easily adjustable pore channels, which endow them with a range of desirable properties. MOFs containing benzene-1,3,5-tricarboxylic acid (BTC) [21] as an organic ligand have been widely studied due to the low cost of this ligand, their simple preparation, and their good stabilities. For example, Shahbakhsh et al. [22] prepared a Cu-BTC MOF that was subsequently used to modify carbon paste for sensing 4-nitrophenol.

Over time, researchers have found that MOFs can, therefore, be regarded as ideal platforms for loading ECL materials and developing MOF composites with extraordinary properties [23,24]. The combination of MOFs with luminescent reagents was therefore expected to allow the construction of high-performance ECL reagents. Due to their large specific surface areas, good electrical conductivities, excellent catalytic performances, and suitable electron transfer efficiencies, MOFs can be loaded with luminescence reagents to enhance the luminescence responses of ECL reagents. In this context, Du. et al. [25] combined CdS quantum dots with MOF-5 to obtain stable enhanced materials for constructing immunosensors. However, the combination of C-dots and MOFs to construct ECL reagent sensors for thiamethoxam detection is yet to be reported.

Thus, we herein propose a novel enzyme-free ECL platform for the ultrasensitive detection of thiamethoxam. This system is based on an innovative ECL composite (i.e., an Fe/Zn-homophthalic acid MOF, Fe/Zn-BTC), which is loaded with C-dots (Fe/Zn-BTC@C-dots) ( Fig. 1). Initially, the Fe/Zn-BTC@C-dots are synthesised and used to modify a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) surface bound with chitosan. Subsequently, a single-chain DNA aptamer of thiamethoxam is bound to the surface of the nanomaterial using a cross-linking agent as a recognition element to construct a biosensor that can recognise thiamethoxam. Due to the high specific surface area and fast electron transfer rate of Fe/Zn-BTC, large numbers of C-dots can be loaded on to the MOF. In addition, the metal centre of the MOF is expected to exhibit electrocatalytic activity, which should allow amplification of the light intensity of the C-dots to improve the sensor sensitivity. Furthermore, the ability of the DNA aptamer to specifically identify the sensor should lead to a good selectivity toward thiamethoxam.

The sucrose, sulfuric acid, polyethylene glycol-200, FeCl3, ZnCl2, 1,3,5-benzenetricarboxylic acid (H3BTC), ethylene glycol, terephthalaldehyde, Tris, and thiamethoxam standards were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). The thiamethoxam oxazine DNA adapter body with a sequence of 5′-NH2-TATGTTCTTAACTGGTCGTCCTGTGAGCCGA TCACTAGATAATTAGGAT-3′ was purchased from Shanghai Biotechnology Co., Ltd, China. The 0.02 mol/L Tris-HCl solution was prepared by dissolving Tris (0.24 g) in deionised water (100 mL), and adjusting the pH to 8.0 using a 0.01 mol/L HCl solution. Unless specified otherwise, all reagents were analytically pure, and deionised water (18.2 MΩ•cm) was used throughout.

The ECL assay was performed using an MIP-E electrochemical analysis system equipped with a multifunctional luminescence detector (Xi'an Yes Analytical Instruments Co., Ltd.) and a three-electrode system (i.e., an Fe/Zn-MOF@C-dots/aptamer-modified GCE electrode, an Ag/AgCl reference electrode, and a platinum wire counter electrode). Electrochemical detection was performed using a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instruments Co., Ltd., China) equipped with the same three-electrode system. The morphologies of the composites were analysed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Zeiss, Germany), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed with a JEM-2000EX microscope (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan; www.rbdzz.poobuy.com), and crystallographic analysis of the composites was performed using an Ultima IV polycrystalline X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Rigaku Co., Ltd., Japan). Elemental analysis of the composites was performed using an ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo Scientific, Inc., USA).

Using the method described in our previous study [26], the C-dots were synthesised by initially mixing sucrose (0.25 g), deionised water (2 mL), sulfuric acid (0.25 mL), and polyethylene glycol-200 (6 mL) in a 10 mL beaker and subjecting this mixture to sonication for 15 min. Subsequently, the sonicated mixture was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min to remove any undissolved materials. After dialysis, the pH was adjusted to 7.4 to obtain the desired C-dots suspension. As per previous literature techniques [27,28], the Fe/Zn-BTC component was synthesised by initially adding FeCl3 (0.81 g), ZnCl2 (0.68 g), and H3BTC (2.00 g) slowly to a solvent mixture containing deionised water (150 mL) and ethylene glycol (50 mL). The mixed solution was continuously stirred for 1.5 h prior to the reaction at 280 °C for 2 h. After this time, an aliquot (3 mL) of the prepared C-dots suspension was added, and the reaction was allowed to proceed for a further 2 h. The mixture was then cooled to room temperature, subjected to centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 5 min, washed with anhydrous ethanol (150 mL), and centrifuged once again. After repeating this operation three times, the obtained solid was dried under vacuum at 60 °C to obtain the desired Fe/Zn-BTC. To investigate the luminescence properties of the Fe/Zn-BTC@C-dots, Fe/Zn-BTC was prepared without the addition of C-dots, Fe-BTC@C-dots were prepared without the addition of ZnCl2, and Zn-BTC@C-dots were prepared without the addition of FeCl3 for comparison. The Fe/BTC was prepared without the addition of ZnCl2 and C-dots.

The GCE was sonicated in anhydrous ethanol and ultrapure water for 5 min prior to immersion in a Tris-HCl solution (0.02 mol/L, pH 8.2, containing 0.05 g chitosan and 0.06 g Fe/Zn-BTC) and subjected to electrodeposition for 300 s. The electrodes were then allowed to dry at 25 °C to obtain the desired Fe/Zn-BTC@C-dots. Subsequently, the GCE was immersed in a 2% terephthalaldehyde solution (0.02 g terephthalaldehyde dissolved in 1 mL deionised water) for 0.5 h prior to immersion in the 1.0 μmol/L DNA aptamer solution for 1 h. During this time, the aptamer was immobilised to obtain the Fe/Zn-BTC@C-dots/aptamer GCE-modified sensor.

A 0.05 mol/L solution of K4[Fe(CN)6]/K3[Fe(CN)6] (containing 0.1 mol/L KCl) was prepared as the probe for the electrochemical method. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was performed over a potential scan range of − 0.20 to + 0.6 V with a scan rate of 50 mV/s and an amplitude of 50 mV. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed at a potential of 0.19 V, using an AC voltage of 5 mV in the frequency range of 100 mHz to 100 kHz. The signal was detected after allowing the sensor to adsorb thiamethoxam from solutions of different concentration over a period of 12 min. ECL detection was performed by placing the electrodes in 0.01 mol/L Tris-HCl buffer (pH 8.2, containing 25 μL of H2O2 with a mass fraction of 30%) and using a three-electrode system to determine the ECL intensity. For these measurements, a scan range of 0 to + 0.8 V (vs. SCE) was employed along with a sweep rate of 100 mV/s. The photomultiplier used a high voltage of 800 V, a sampling rate of 10 T/S, a magnification of 3, and a measurement time of 90 s

The water samples (10 mL) and plant-derived samples (25 g) were placed in beakers (250 mL) and homogenised in the presence of acetonitrile (50 mL) at a high speed for 3 min. The resulting mixture was filtered through medium-speed qualitative filter paper and thoroughly shaken after the addition of NaCl (5 g). After resting for 30 min, an aliquot (10 mL) of the supernatant was taken and dried under vacuum at 45 °C on a rotary evaporator then dissolved in a Tris-HCl solution (25 mL, 0.02 mol/L, pH 8.2) for measurement.

The prepared Fe/Zn-BTC was characterised by SEM, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), XRD, and XPS. The SEM images are shown in Fig. 2, wherein it can be seen that Fe-BTC has a regular hexahedral morphology (Fig. 2A), Zn-BTC has a hexagonal polygonal structure, and the C-dots are spherical with a diameter of ∼20 nm (Fig. 2B). However, two forms of mixtures were obtained, wherein Zn was uniformly distributed on the Fe-BTC surface in a spherical plane, and the C-dots were distributed on the Zn-BTC surface. This result is consistent with the TEM images (Fig. S1 in Supplementary Material), which showed that the Fe-BTC particles are relatively large, and that Zn-BTC species and the C-dots sequentially cover the Fe-BTC surface. In the elemental mapping diagram (Fig. S2), it can be seen that the distribution of Zn and C overlaps with that of Fe, which further confirms that the Zn-BTC species and the C-dots are successively overlaid on the surfaces of the Fe-BTC particles. In addition, the XRD pattern of the prepared Fe/Zn-BTC@C-dots specimen is shown in Fig. 2C. In this pattern, the wide peak at a 2θ value of 8.6° was attributed to the uncertain crystal surface of C(002), which belonged to the C-dots. The appearance of sharp diffraction peaks corresponding to Zn-BTC and Fe-BTC indicated the high crystallinity of the prepared Fe/Zn-BTC, which was consistent with the simulated results (Fig. 2C) and with the results described previously for Zn-based and Fe-based MOFs [29,30]. Furthermore, the peaks at 2θ = 7.2, 10.3, 12.6, 16.4, 17.9, 24.5, and 26.6° were attributed to the crystal surface of Zn, while those at 2θ = 9.4, 14.5, 13.4, 17.4, 16.5, 19.0, and 25.9° were attributed to the crystal surface of Fe. Moreover, the XPS profiles are shown in Fig. 2D, wherein strong signals corresponding to C, O, Fe, and Zn at 285 eV (C 1 s), 533 eV (O 1 s), 712 eV (Fe 2p), 1022 eV (Zn 2p), and 1045 eV (Zn 2p), thereby confirming that the Fe/Zn-BTC@C-dots contained only these four elements. The above results therefore indicate that the Fe/Zn-BTC@C-dots were successfully synthesised.

As the binding of nanomaterials to the electrode surface affects the K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] solution at the sensing interface, a 3 × 10−4 mol/L solution of K3[Fe(CN)6] and K4[Fe(CN)6] was selected as the probe. The resulting sensor was then evaluated using CV and EIS. As shown in Fig. 3A, the CV response of the probe was enhanced when the Fe/Zn-BTC@C-dots were used to modify the GCE surface, and this was attributed to their excellent electrical conductivity and large specific surface area, which in turn increased the number of probe responses on the electrode surface (curves a and b). The CV response of the probe decreased when the DNA aptamer was bound to the electrode surface (curve c), and the CV response decreased further upon the capture of the target thiamethoxam molecule by the aptamer (curve d). This second reduction in the CV response was attributed to the poor conductivity of the DNA aptamer–thiamethoxam structure, which led to reduced electron transport.

The amplification effect of the Fe-/Zn-BTC on the ECL response signal of the C-dots was subsequently investigated. For this, C-dots-, Fe-/Zn-BTC-, Fe-BTC@C-dots-, Zn-BTC@C-dots-, and Fe-/Zn-BTC@C-dots-modified electrode sensors were prepared, and their ECL responses were measured. As shown in Fig. 4A, the C-dots displayed a clear and stable ECL response signal on the glassy carbon electrode (curve a), whereas the Fe/Zn-MOF species showed no response signal (curve b). In terms of the composite nanomaterials, both Fe-BTC and Zn-BTC effectively amplified the ECL response signal of the C-dots (curves c and d), while in the Fe/Zn-BTC@C-dots composite, Fe/Zn-BTC significantly amplified the ECL response signal of the C-dots (curve e). Compared to the single metal-based MOF materials, Fe/Zn-BTC exhibited a superior amplification effect and amplified the ECL response signal of the C-dots by ∼11 times.

The mechanism of ECL production and quenching was subsequently considered. More specifically, the C-dots can become excited to generate the C-dots+• radical cation, while the H2O2 reaction can accept an electron to form a radical (•OH) and an anion (OH−), the latter of which reacts with the C-dots+• species to generate excited C-dots* . When the excited C-dots* return to the ground state, ECL is generated. In the presence of thiamethoxam, an anionic nitryl radical intermediate is then formed at the electrode, which binds to the C-dots+• species to block combination with the OH− anions, thereby quenching the ECL signal [31,32]. It was therefore speculated that the signal amplification observed for the Fe/Zn-BTC@C-dots was due to the large surface area of the material and the greater number of loaded C-dots, which ultimately increased the electron transfer rate. More importantly, it was found that the electron-coupling effect of the bimetal in the MOF material further improved the electrocatalytic activity [33] whilst also effectively catalysing the oxidation reaction of H2O2. As shown in Fig. 4B, the oxidation current of H2O2 on the C-dots modified electrodes was small (curve a), while those on the Fe-BTC@C-dots and Zn-BTC@C-dots modified electrodes were increased (curve b and curve c), indicating that both Fe-BTC and Zn-BTC can catalyse the reaction of H2O2. However, on the Fe/Zn-BTC@C-dots modified electrode, the oxidation current of H2O2 was greatly amplified (curve d), which demonstrated that the bimetallic centre can indeed effectively catalyse H2O2 oxidation to produce greater amounts of OH−, participate in the ECL reaction, and enhance the luminescence intensity.

During thiamethoxam detection by the sensor, the pH of the Tris-HCl buffer solution, the amount of H2O2, and the reaction times of DNA capture of thiamethoxam adsorption are known to influence the ECL signal intensity. Thus, these factors were optimised to determine the most appropriate conditions for sensing. As shown in Fig. S3A, the ECL response of the sensor gradually increased upon increasing the buffer pH from 7.2 to 8.2, beyond which point, the response decreased once again. A pH of 8.2 was therefore selected as optimal for the Tris-HCl buffer. In addition, as shown in Fig. S3B, the ECL signal increased upon increasing the amount of added H2O2 from 5 to 25 μL, even though a subsequent decrease was observed at higher H2O2 volumes; a volume of 25 μL H2O2 was therefore selected as optimal in this case. In terms of the adsorption time (see Fig. S3C), an increasing amount of thiamethoxam was adsorbed onto the sensor over time, which burst the ECL signal. As a result, a time of 12 min was selected as optimal.

Under optimal conditions, the sensor was placed in different concentrations of thiamethoxam solution. During the capture of the target molecule, the electrodes were placed in the luminescent substrate to measure the luminescence intensity and determine the ECL quenching value of the sensor before and after the capture of thiamethoxam. As shown in Fig. 5A, upon increasing the thiamethoxam concentration, the degree of ECL quenching increased continuously, with a linear relationship being observed between the decrease in the ECL (△I) and the thiamethoxam concentration (c) over a range of 5.0 × 10−11 to 5000 × 10−11 mol/L. The linear regression equation of the working curve was determined to be △I = 1.25c(10−11 mol/L) + 146.9 (Fig. 5B), with a correlation coefficient of r = 0.9985 and a detection limit of 9.81 × 10−12 mol/L (limit of detection = 3σ/m, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank and m is the slope of the calibration plot). Compared with previously reported methods for the detection of thiamethoxam (Table S1), the developed sensor exhibits a higher sensitivity and a wider linear range.

The selective recognition ability of thiamethoxam by the developed sensor is key to determining its practical applicability. Thus, the selective recognition performance of the thiamethoxam sensor was verified by evaluating its performance in the presence of other pesticide molecules. As shown in Fig. 6, the ECL intensity (I
0 ) was measured after the adsorption of 5.0 × 10−9 mol/L thiamethoxam, as were the corresponding ECL intensities (I
1 ) after mixing 5.0 × 10−9 mol/L thiamethoxam with 5.0 × 10−7 mol/L of various pesticides, namely imidacloprid, acetamiprid, tiothiamine, fipronil, oxytetracycline, chloramphenicol, atrazine, chlorpyrifos, methyl parathion, acetamiprid, nitenpyram, and thiacloprid. The relative deviation (RD%) between the two ECL strengths in each case was calculated using the expression RD% = [(I
0 − I
1 )/I
0 × 100]. As can be seen from the figure, all RD% values are < 5%. The ECL response signals of the different interferents on the sensor were then investigated further. Since these interferents cannot be recognised by the sensor, they have no quenching effect on the ECL signal, and so the ECL signal of the sensor remains relatively constant (see Fig. S4). These results therefore illustrate the excellent selective recognition performance of the developed thiamethoxam sensor.

To determine the practical applicability of this sensor for thiamethoxam detection, its stability and reproducibility were investigated. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of the ECL was calculated as 0.35% after 10 consecutive ECL determinations following the re-adsorption of 5.0 × 10−9 mol/L trichlorfon on the same sensor (Fig. S5). Subsequently, ten sensors were prepared according to the same experimental procedure, and their ECL values were measured following the re-adsorption of 5.0 × 10−9 mol/L thiamethoxam. In this case, a relative standard deviation of 0.29% was obtained from the ECL determinations carried out using the 10 prepared sensors (Fig. S6). In addition, the stability of the sensors was studied by placing them for a prolonged period, and the ECL was measured after the re-adsorption of 5.0 × 10−9 mol/L thiamethoxam over 8 min. Furthermore, after storage of the sensor in a moist refrigerated atmosphere at 4 °C, the ECL was measured after 15 d, and the relative deviation (RD%) was calculated to be 1.05%. The signal intensity decreased to only 88.4% of the original signal after 30 d (Fig. S7), thereby indicating the outstanding stability of the developed sensor.

To further demonstrate the usefulness of the sensor, it was employed for the practical detection of thiamethoxam in banana, cowpea, and other vegetable samples. Thus, the sensor was placed in the desired sample solution for 8 min, its ECL intensity was measured, and spiked recovery experiments were performed. All samples were analysed using high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS, Table 1) to obtain recoveries ranging from 90.5% to 109.3% and relative standard deviations of < 5%.

In this study, a novel strategy based on the Fe/Zn-BTC-enhanced electrochemiluminescence of C-dots was proposed and employed to construct a DNA-adapted electrochemiluminescence sensor for the sensitive detection of the pesticide thiamethoxam. The prepared Fe/Zn-BTC@C-dots composites were employed as the electrochemiluminescence reagent, and the electrochemiluminescence was significantly enhanced compared to that of the C-dots. In addition, the DNA aptamers were used as specific recognition elements for the target thiamethoxam molecule, which led to an effective improvement in the selection performance of the sensor. Overall, this sensor exhibited remarkable reproducibility, good selectivity, and long-term stability, thereby indicating its potential for use in the detection of thiamethoxam residues in agricultural products and environments of origin.

Shuhuai Li: Methodology, Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing–review & editing. Yan Deng: Formal analysis, Writing-Review & Editing. Chaohai Pang: Resources, Software. Xionghui Ma: Resources, Formal analysis. Yuwei Wu: Formal analysis, Resources. Mingyue Wang: Software, Funding acquisition. Jianping Li: Methodology, Conceptualization. Zhi Xu: Software, Funding acquisition. Lianming Zhang: Software, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the Hainan Province Science and Technology Special Fund (320QN302 and ZDYF2022XDNY232), the earmarked fund for CARS (CARS-31), the Key Laboratory of Tropical Fruits and Vegetables Quality and Safety for State Market Regulation (ZX-2023002), and the Central Public-interest Scientific Institution Basal Research Fund (1630082020001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2023.134415.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Shuhuai Li received his M.S. degree from College of Chemistry and Bioengineering, Guilin University of Technology in 2013. Currently, he is a associate professor in Analysis and Test Center of Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences, His research interests are electroanalytical chemistry, chemical and biological sensors.
Yan Deng entered Guilin University of Technology to study for a master's degre. He majored in materials and chemicals. He studied under Professor Li Jianping and Shuhuai Li.

Chaohai Pang received his M.S. degree from College of Pharmacy, Guangdong Pharmaceutical University in 2017. He is now a research associate in Analysis and Test Center of Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences. He is currently engaged in research on agricultural product quality and safety.

Xionghui Ma received his M.S. degree from College of Chemistry and Bioengineering, Guilin University of Technology in 2017. He is now a research associate in Analysis and Test Center of Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences. His research interest is electroanalytical chemistry and chemical sensors.

Yuwei Wu received his M.S. degree from Department of Food Science and Nutrition, Hong Kong Polytechnic University in 2020. She is now a research associate in Analysis and Test Center of Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences. Her research interest is electroanalytical chemistry and chemical sensors.

Mingyue Wang received her B.S. degree from Tropical Agricultural University Of South China in 1992. Currently, she is a professor in the Analysis and Testing Center of the Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences. Her research interests is the quality and safety research of agricultural products.

Jianping Li received his Ph.D. degree from Zhejiang University in 2003. Currently, he is a professor in College of Chemistry and Bioengineering, Guilin University of Technology, China. His research interests are electroanalytical chemistry, chemical and biological sensors.

Zhi Xu received his M.S. degree from Tropical Agricultural University Of South China in 2003. Currently, he is a professor in the Analysis and Testing Center of the Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences. His research interests is the quality and safety research of agricultural products.

Lianming Zhang received his Ph.D. degree from Chengdu University of Technology in 2017. Currently, he is an associate professor in College of Chemistry and Bioengineering, Guilin University of Technology, China. His research interests are electroanalytical chemistry, chemical and biological sensors.
